title: Whakairo Awa: Carving the River's Story / Te Whakairo o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
subject: Arts
culturalContext: Students will explore the art of whakairo (carving) through the story of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (The Stream of the Fantail), a significant waterway that flows through our rohe. They will learn how our tūpuna used carving to record history, with specific focus on the pūrākau of Kahungunu's journey along this awa and how the fantail (pīwakawaka) became kaitiaki of these waters.
learningObjectives: Create a collaborative whakairo panel that visually narrates the pūrākau of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku using traditional Māori design elements
Analyze and explain how at least three specific patterns (kowhaiwhai, pakati, unaunahi) in their artwork connect to the awa's story and environment
Collaborate effectively in a rōpū to plan, design, and execute a meaningful art piece that demonstrates understanding of ngā uara Māori (Māori values)
Present their completed artwork to whānau and community members, explaining the cultural significance and artistic choices made

activities.title: He Hīkoi Awa: Walking with the River
activities.description: Students will walk the banks of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku with local kaumātua, listening to the pūrākau of the awa and identifying natural forms, patterns, and materials that inspire traditional carving designs. They will sketch observations and collect natural materials for reference.

activities.title: Ngā Tauira Taketake: Traditional Pattern Workshop
activities.description: Working with a local carver from Ngāti Kahungunu, students will learn fundamental whakairo patterns (kowhaiwhai, pakati, unaunahi) and their meanings. They will practice these patterns on soap or soft wood, understanding how each pattern tells part of a larger story.

activities.title: Te Whakaaturanga o te Awa: Designing Our Story
activities.description: In small rōpū, students will design a collaborative whakairo panel that tells the story of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. Each group must incorporate at least three traditional patterns and represent key elements from the pūrākau (the fantail, the water, Kahungunu's journey).

activities.title: Te Mahi Whakairo: Carving Our Narrative
activities.description: Using appropriate materials (wood or contemporary alternatives), students will bring their designs to life through carving. They will work collaboratively, dividing tasks according to individual strengths while maintaining the integrity of their shared vision.

activities.title: Whakanuia te Mahi: Celebration and Presentation
activities.description: Students will prepare and present their completed whakairo panels to whānau and community members, explaining the story, patterns, and cultural significance. The artworks will be displayed along the college's wharenui or community center.

resources: Local kaumātua from Ngāti Kahungunu to share pūrākau of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Master carver from Toi Kahungunu for pattern workshops and technical guidance
Digital archive: 'Ngā Kōrero o Te Awa' from Hastings District Council libraries
Traditional carving tools and appropriate materials (wood, carving chisels, safety equipment)

tasks: Completed collaborative whakairo panel demonstrating technical skill and cultural understanding
Individual written reflection explaining the cultural significance of three patterns used
Group presentation to community explaining the artwork's narrative and creative process
Peer assessment of collaboration and contribution to group mahi

nzcAlignment: Understanding the Arts in Context: UC-1 Investigate the forms and styles of traditional Māori arts
Developing Practical Knowledge: PK-1 Explore and use art-making conventions, applying knowledge of elements and principles
Developing Ideas: DI-1 Develop and revisit visual ideas in response to cultural and community contexts
Communicating and Interpreting: CI-1 Share the ideas, feelings, and stories communicated by their own and others' objects and images
